subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter is built around discovering and describing patterns in whole numbers: the Idli-Vada and Jump Jackpot games in section 5.1 surface patterns in multiples and factors; the Sieve of Eratosthenes in section 5.2 is explicitly a pattern-based procedure for identifying prime numbers (prime numbers are named in C-1.2's own exemplar list); and divisibility tests in section 5.5 derive rules by observing last-digit or last-two-digit patterns in multiples of 2, 4, 5, 8, and 10.

Sections 5.2 through 5.4 systematically explore distinct sets of whole numbers — primes, composites, co-primes — and their properties, directly exercising C-1.4's 'explore and understand sets of numbers and their properties' strand. Prime factorisation (section 5.4) establishes that every number greater than 1 has a unique prime decomposition, revealing a structural relationship among these sets; co-primality and divisibility tests then apply these properties to solve concrete problems.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 5.4 explicitly requires deductive reasoning in elementary number theory: the Anshu–Guna dialogue exposes a logical flaw (partial factorisation does not reveal all common factors), and the chapter then proves that absence of common prime factors is sufficient to establish co-primality and that divisibility is equivalent to the prime factorisation of the divisor being contained in that of the dividend. Section 5.5 similarly asks students to verify three-statement logical arguments about the last-two-digit and last-three-digit divisibility rules for 4 and 8.

The Prime Puzzle in section 5.6 (E-49) challenges students to fill a grid with prime numbers subject to simultaneous row-product and column-product constraints, requiring creative search strategies with no single prescribed path. Open-ended exercises such as E-12 (finding number pairs for the Idli-Vada game given that the first common multiple exceeds 50) and E-48 (explaining why each number in a box is special) similarly reward students who discover their own solution routes.

Section 5.2 explicitly names Eratosthenes, situates his sieve method approximately 2,200 years ago in Greek civilisation, and credits him with developing the algorithm. A sidebar also references Euclid's proof of the infinitude of primes as a result students will encounter in a later class, placing the chapter's number-theory content within a recognisable tradition of mathematical discovery across history.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to understand the structure of whole numbers by discovering patterns among multiples and factors, identifying and classifying primes and composites, performing prime factorisation, and applying divisibility rules — progressively deepening from pattern recognition to deductive reasoning about number properties.
